name: Type I Single-Panel Codebook

lineage.adds: a full oversampled beam grid and ranks up to 8, for arrays of 4 or more ports

tagline: Point at the single best direction the channel is coming from, then rotate the two antenna polarizations to line up — the cheapest general-purpose report in the whole family.
overview: Think of a lighthouse operator who, instead of describing the sea in detail, just points a single beam at the brightest ship on the horizon. Type I Single-Panel is that lighthouse: the phone (**UE**) looks at the radio channel and reports **one direction** — a beam picked from a dense, oversampled compass of possible directions — plus a small phase adjustment to line up the two antenna polarizations. There is one shared direction for the whole frequency band (**wideband**), and only a cheap per-frequency-unit tweak on top.

The beam comes from a **DFT (discrete Fourier transform) beam grid**: a virtual compass with far more headings than there are physical antennas, built by oversampling the array's natural resolution. The UE reports two small indices, $i_{1,1}$ and $i_{1,2}$, that pick a point on this grid — like naming a compass bearing to the nearest degree instead of the nearest cardinal direction. A base station (**gNB**) with a dual-polarized array also needs to know how to combine the two polarizations coherently; that is the co-phasing index $i_2$, refreshed every frequency unit.

Type I supports **ranks 1 through 8** (how many independent data streams travel at once), with the beam construction growing richer at higher rank: rank 1 is a single beam and a phase; ranks 2-4 add a second, offset companion beam; ranks 5-8 combine three or four fixed beams with prescribed sign patterns. A **codebookMode** switch (Mode 1 vs Mode 2) trades a coarser wideband beam for a finer per-subband refinement, or vice versa — a direct knob on the accuracy/overhead trade-off.

Because the whole report boils down to a handful of small integers, Type I is by far the cheapest general-purpose codebook here — tens of bits, not hundreds. The price is fidelity: a single beam is a poor description of a channel that arrives from many different directions at once (a *rich* or *scattered* channel). Type I shines when the channel is spatially sparse — dominated by one or two clear paths — and struggles as richness grows, which is exactly the gap the Type II family (next in this catalog) was built to close.

howItWorks.title: UE measures the channel
howItWorks.body: The phone estimates the channel from CSI-RS (channel state information reference signal) pilots across all configured antenna ports and frequency units.

howItWorks.title: UE searches the beam grid by rate, not by energy
howItWorks.body: Unlike the Type II family, Type I scores every candidate beam (and, at rank 2-4, every companion-beam offset) and every co-phasing state directly by the data rate it would achieve at a nominal SNR (signal-to-noise ratio) — an exhaustive rate-maximizing search rather than an energy-capture heuristic.

howItWorks.title: UE fixes one wideband beam direction
howItWorks.body: The winning beam indices ($i_{1,1}$, $i_{1,2}$, and for rank 2-4 the companion offset $i_{1,3}$) are chosen once for the entire reporting band — this is the wideband part of the report.

howItWorks.title: UE refines per-subband co-phasing
howItWorks.body: For each frequency unit, the UE independently picks the best co-phasing state $i_2$ that rotates the two antenna polarizations into alignment (and, in codebookMode 2 at low rank, a fine beam offset folded into the same index).

howItWorks.title: Report travels over the uplink
howItWorks.body: The compact index set — a few wideband integers plus one small index per frequency unit — is sent back to the gNB as the PMI (precoding matrix indicator).

howItWorks.title: gNB reconstructs and transmits
howItWorks.body: The gNB looks up the exact DFT beam(s) and applies the reported phase rotations using a fixed, standardized formula, then transmits with that precoder.

whatIsReported.plain: The horizontal beam direction, shared by the whole frequency band.
whatIsReported.detail: A wideband index into the oversampled horizontal beam grid (size $G_1 = N_1 O_1$). It is always reported.

whatIsReported.plain: The vertical beam direction, shared by the whole frequency band.
whatIsReported.detail: A wideband index into the oversampled vertical beam grid (size $G_2 = N_2 O_2$). Omitted entirely for 1-D arrays where $N_2 = 1$.

whatIsReported.plain: Which offset positions the companion beam(s) for multi-layer reports.
whatIsReported.detail: Reported only for ranks 2-4; selects the fixed offset $(k_1, k_2)$ of the second beam relative to the first, from a small standardized table.

whatIsReported.plain: The per-frequency-unit co-phasing (and, in Mode 2, a fine beam refinement).
whatIsReported.detail: One small index per PMI frequency unit that rotates the two antenna polarizations into phase alignment; in codebookMode 2 at rank 1-2 it also carries a fine offset that sharpens the wideband beam.

parametersExplained.plain: codebookMode 1 reports one coarse wideband beam; mode 2 reports a coarser wideband beam but refines it every subband.
parametersExplained.guidance: Pick mode 1 for the cheapest report; pick mode 2 only when the channel's direction is expected to be finer than the base grid resolution and you can afford wider per-subband indices.

parametersExplained.plain: How many simultaneous data streams (1-8) to describe.
parametersExplained.guidance: Higher ranks reuse the same beam machinery with fixed sign/offset patterns rather than independently optimized directions — expect diminishing fidelity gains as rank grows.

strengths: By far the cheapest general-purpose report in the catalog — tens of bits, not hundreds.
Reconstruction is a closed-form lookup and rotation, trivial for the gNB to compute.
Works well when the channel is spatially sparse (few dominant propagation paths).
Scales to ranks up to 8 using the same beam-grid machinery.
limitations: A single beam direction cannot represent a channel that arrives from many angles at once.
Higher ranks are built from fixed offsets and sign patterns, not independently optimized directions.
Per-subband adaptation is limited to a phase tweak, not a new direction.
Codebook subset restriction is only approximated at per-beam granularity in this implementation, not the exact spec group-bitmap rule.
whenToUse: Use Type I when feedback overhead must be minimal and the channel is reasonably sparse — for example a line-of-sight or lightly scattered link — or as the cheap baseline against which every richer codebook's accuracy-per-bit is measured.

mathHighlight.caption: At rank 1 the precoder is one DFT beam, duplicated onto both polarizations with a QPSK phase rotation between them, normalized so the total transmit power stays fixed.
